question: [File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered ETHEREUMTRANSACTION that sets the sender's evmAddress to a value matching a victim account's evmAddress, causing findByEvmAddress() to return the victim's entity when the attacker's EVM address is queried, leading to incorrect attribution of transactions and balance queries?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the native query's use of positional parameter ?1 in 'select * from entity where alias = ?1 and deleted is not true' by submitting a base32-encoded alias that, after decoding, contains special PostgreSQL bytea escape sequences, causing unexpected query behavior when the alias was stored via a tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getByEvmAddressAndType() calls findById() for long-form addresses without checking the evm_address column, allowing an attacker who tampered a CryptoCreate transaction to set an entity's ID to a predictable value, then use a crafted long-form EVM address to directly retrieve that entity by ID?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOCREATEACCOUNT transaction that sets the alias field to a value identical to an existing account's alias, causing findByAlias() to return the first matching entity (due to database ordering), potentially returning the wrong account and leaking its data?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CONTRACTUPDATEINSTANCE transaction that changes a contract's evmAddress to match a victim account's evmAddress, causing findByEvmAddress() to return the contract entity when queried with the victim's address, and the EntityType.ACCOUNT filter in getByEvmAddressAndType() to return empty, effectively making the victim account unreachable via EVM address lookup?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CrudRepository-inherited save() method exposed via @GraphQlRepository annotation to directly write a tampered entity record with a crafted alias, bypassing transaction processing entirely and causing findByAlias() to return the injected entity?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the CrudRepository-inherited delete() method potentially exposed via @GraphQlRepository to delete an entity record, causing findByEvmAddress() to return empty for a victim account's EVM address, effectively making the account invisible to EVM address lookups?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered SCHEDULECREATE transaction that schedules a CryptoCreate with a crafted alias, and upon execution via SCHEDULESIGN, causes findByAlias() to return the newly created entity with the attacker-controlled alias, allowing the attacker to predict and pre-register alias lookups?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOCREATEACCOUNT transaction that sets both alias and evmAddress to attacker-controlled values, causing findByEvmAddress() to return the new entity when queried with the attacker's chosen EVM address, and then use the returned entity's key field to impersonate the account in subsequent operations?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum byte length check on the alias parameter in findByAlias() to submit an extremely large decoded alias byte array, causing a database query with an oversized bytea parameter that triggers a denial-of-service condition affecting all concurrent findByAlias() queries?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that decodeEvmAddress() strips only the '0x' prefix (not '0X') and then hex-decodes, by submitting an EVM address with '0X' prefix that is not stripped, causing Hex.decodeHex() to fail with an IllegalArgumentException that leaks internal error details through the CustomExceptionResolver?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOTRANSFER transaction that modifies the balance of an account whose alias is known, then immediately query findByAlias() to observe the updated balance before the mirror node has fully processed the transaction, creating a timing-based information disclosure about pending transaction effects?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered ETHEREUMTRANSACTION that creates a contract at a specific EVM address, then query findByEvmAddress() with that address to retrieve the contract entity, and use the returned key and memo fields to infer sensitive deployment parameters that were embedded in the tampered transaction?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the native query uses 'deleted is not true' (which returns rows where deleted IS NULL or deleted IS FALSE) rather than 'deleted = false', allowing a tampered transaction that sets deleted=NULL to keep an entity visible in findByAlias() results even after a CryptoDelete transaction has been processed?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the native query uses 'deleted is not true' (which includes NULL) rather than 'deleted = false', allowing a tampered CONTRACTDELETEINSTANCE transaction that sets deleted=NULL to keep a contract visible in findByEvmAddress() results, enabling the attacker to observe the contract's state after deletion?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOUPDATEACCOUNT transaction that sets the memo field of a victim account to contain sensitive data (e.g., private key hints), then query findByAlias() with the victim's alias to retrieve the tampered memo field, causing information disclosure?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOUPDATEACCOUNT transaction that changes the key field of a victim account to an attacker-controlled key, then query findByEvmAddress() to retrieve the updated key field, confirming the tampering was successful and using the key information to plan further attacks?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered SCHEDULESIGN transaction that executes a scheduled CryptoDelete, causing the target account's deleted flag to be set, and then exploit the 'deleted is not true' NULL ambiguity in findByAlias() if the importer sets deleted=NULL instead of deleted=true, keeping the account visible in alias lookups?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered TOKENASSOCIATE transaction that associates a token with an account whose evmAddress is known, then query findByEvmAddress() to retrieve the account entity and observe the maxAutomaticTokenAssociations field, inferring the account's token association capacity and planning token-based attacks?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the base32 decoding in decodeBase32() which uses Apache Commons Codec's lenient decoder that silently ignores invalid characters, allowing submission of a malformed base32 string that decodes to a valid alias byte sequence matching an entity written by a tampered transaction, bypassing the @Pattern validation on the alias input field?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the long-form EVM address path in getByEvmAddressAndType() where findById() is called without the 'deleted is not true' filter (unlike findByEvmAddress()), allowing retrieval of a deleted entity whose ID is encoded in a crafted long-form EVM address, bypassing the deletion filter applied in findByEvmAddress()?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CRYPTOCREATEACCOUNT transaction that sets the alias to a value that is a valid base32 encoding of another account's evmAddress bytes, causing findByAlias() to return the new account when queried with the victim's evmAddress encoded as base32, creating an alias-evmAddress confusion attack?

[File: 'File Name: graphql/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/graphql/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Any impacts caused by Tampering with submitted transactions'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[])] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a tampered CONTRACTCREATEINSTANCE transaction that deploys a contract at an EVM address matching a system account (e.g., address 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001), causing findByEvmAddress() to return the contract entity instead of the system account, disrupting system account lookups?
